text:
Products E/W has notched up higher since the escalations restarted. 

It all boils down to the Asian Crude Buying Conundrum. 

In summary, Asia might be underbought for September and runrates may need to come off even with whatever spectacular cracks we may be seeing.

Have a look at the slide and see if it answers your questions. 

#oott
Products E/W overview with Sparta's Live Curves #oott https://t.co/mXcmpeA4ME

The fuel oil market is notoriously difficult and opaque. Lots of guesswork involved and figuring out what component (even crude!) is making sense into the blend pool. 

I'm going to break it all down for you with the help of my esteemed colleagues Hoa and Jay. 

Join us for the webinar for a Singapore-friendly hour on Tuesday 4th August.

Link to register below.

#oott

Excluding any US-Iran talks about Hormuz, China remains the most important factor for oil prices for the next 60 days or so. All eyes on Beijing.

Ex-China Asian oil imports have recovered to normal levels (see 📈⤵️), within the 2023-2025 range. What China does next is crucial. https://t.co/jJTUd5ZCGR

Last week, I wrote this @Opinion column about what China may do next. I see a recovery in oil imports in July and August, but purchases will remain 25% below pre-war levels. Based on my industry soundings, I don't see China stockpiling in the short-term. https://t.co/aspIn1l5eK

Iran appears to be trying to shut down a lifeline of the global oil market -- the "shuttle" tankers that are running from Persian Gulf terminals (mostly UAE ports) to the Gulf of Oman where they rendezvous with other tankers in ship-to-ship operations.  

https://t.co/5PzFVOFmt2

Russia is struggling to sell its crude oil, with floating inventories building up again. Its troubles are compounded by the need to sell overseas a growing amount of barrels as Ukrainian attacks have reduced its domestic refining capacity. 

https://t.co/GJgaEpBr5K

3-2-1 crack spread just hit $68/bbl. 

Congrats Bessent and BOJ 👏

You guys really fucked it up. Now if crude rallies, refining margins won’t fall. You hit the wall sooner and faster. https://t.co/FvtcMTjPXR

Strait of Hormuz over the last 48 hours: Back to the start.

As the war rages on, Fujairah as the main tanker shuttle port is all but dead after the IRGC fired missiles at two VLCCs, the first escalation of its type yesterday.

Even with US protection, it was not enough, and this is going to take ~6 million b/d of flow out of the market.

To further add to my point that the escalation is increasing, this segment from the WSJ article is very concerning:

"There was also concern among shippers that the scope of Iran’s attacks was widening away from the immediate vicinity of the strait after a vessel was targeted off Oman early Tuesday, according to its owner, London-based Stolt-Nielsen. It had not crossed the strait at all and was loaded with Omani chemicals headed to Malaysia, according to Kpler."

We are in no-man's land now. Either Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz or it doesn't. It's all-or-nothing.

I have included the video from @MarineTraffic.

Fujairah is done.

"Sinokor and MSC executives are concerned the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps will continue to attack their ships to try to shut down the shuttle market, said people familiar with the matter. At least two captains on their tankers have refused to go through the strait in recent days."
Japan refiners to diversify crude sources, explore support for Hormuz bypass routes #oott https://t.co/BggbwS6599

The Strait of Hormuz is effectively off-limits to commercial shipping in the near term unless the US-Iran conflict can be resolved, the head of a major Japanese business lobby said on Wednesday.

Masahiro Okafuji, chair of the Japan Foreign Trade Council, said most vessels with a connection to Japan had exited the strait prior to the latest flare-up in hostilities. For the time being, he added, ships were now likely to avoid the wider region, including the Red Sea.

“No one would go there, because it’s dangerous,” Okafuji, who is also chief executive officer of major Japanese trading house Itochu Corp., said during a media conference in Tokyo. He didn’t specify whether he was referring to Japanese vessels or shipping in general.
https://t.co/2hDxvxMGll

Ukrainian forces hit multiple Russian vessels in the Black Sea overnight as Kyiv expands the scope of its naval attacks against Moscow.

“The first round of the naval battle is over” after Ukraine hit more than 100 Russia-linked ships in the Sea of Azov in recent days, drone unit commander Robert Brovdi said in a statement on Telegram. “Now, the Black Sea.”

On Wednesday, Ukraine attacked 17 oil tankers, two gas carriers and one tug boat there, Brovdi said, adding that he’ll provide an official report and video evidence later. Bloomberg couldn’t independently verify the number of affected vessels.
#oott
https://t.co/e30jNZFnlu

TRUMP: Ultimately, we'll hit energy targets in Iran. Next week comes the bridges. We're going to knock out all of their power plants. We'll knock out all of their bridges unless they get to the table and negotiate - Fox https://t.co/BViFUwjD3M

Chicago and Paris #wheat futures have surged around 10% over the past week, lifting the September Paris milling wheat contract to a 16-month high near EUR 226 per tonne. The rally reflects a combination of acute geopolitical disruption and an increasingly tight global supply outlook.
The immediate catalyst was a sharp escalation in military attacks around the Sea of Azov, with strikes targeting Russian and Ukrainian grain export infrastructure. Temporary disruptions to shipping through the Don-Azov Channel and renewed attacks on Black Sea export ports raised concerns about exports from a region that accounts for a significant share of global wheat trade.
However, the market was already on edge. Severe drought in the US and Canada, deteriorating crop prospects across Europe, and the USDA's latest downward revisions to production and global ending stocks had already tightened the supply outlook. At the same time, poor European corn crops are expected to boost feed wheat demand, further increasing pressure on available supplies.
In short, weather-related production risks created a highly sensitive market, while the renewed disruption to Black Sea grain logistics provided the spark that triggered the latest leg higher in wheat prices.

A reminder that the real stress in energy markets is not in crude oil but in refined products. While Brent has rallied back above USD 80, diesel, gasoil and jet fuel continue to command exceptionally strong premiums as low inventories, refinery disruptions and peak seasonal demand tighten product availability.

As the charts highlight, crack spreads across the US, Europe and Asia have surged to multi-year highs, signalling that refiners - not crude producers - are currently capturing the bulk of the value. Refining margins are now approaching historic levels, creating a major windfall for operators at a time when global middle-distillate inventories remain critically low and supply chains continue to face disruption. Until refined product stocks begin to rebuild or demand eases seasonally, these elevated margins are likely to keep the energy complex well supported, even if crude supply itself remains relatively comfortable.

#Commodities:

#CrudeOil prices trade higher for a third day after Trump threatened further strikes on Iran, hours after the US resumed its blockade on Iran’s shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, Trump’s U-turn on plans to impose a 20% charge on shipments through the waterway briefly sent prices lower before the resumption of attacks, including Houthi groups firing ballistic missiles and drones on Saudi Arabia added to concerns about supply from the region. Focus on US stockpiles amid the release of weekly data from the EIA.

#Gold rallied to USD 4,100 after softer-than-expected US CPI data raised expectations for a less aggressive Federal Reserve. However, the move quickly reversed, with prices slipping back toward USD 4,030 after Fed Chair Kevin Warsh signalled monetary policy may still need to be tightened. At the same time, renewed gains in oil prices and fresh US strikes against Iran revived inflation concerns, shifting market focus back toward the risk of higher interest rates rather than the longer-term, gold-supportive economic fallout from a sustained energy shock. For now, gold is sending no clear directional signal, with the yellow metal continuing to consolidate following a prolonged and deep correction.
